title: The Blind Spots in Chinese Military Studies
gist: RAND/Asia Society China analyst Lyle Morris argues that authoritative PLA doctrinal sources are drying up and being restricted just as China modernizes with AI, autonomous systems, and hypersonics, forcing Western war planners into mirror-imaging and flawed assessments — and proposes reviving the Cold War-era Foreign Broadcast Information Service as a public open-source repository housed at the Library of Congress.

themes.theme: Drying up of PLA doctrinal sources

insights.claim: Authoritative PLA doctrinal texts — chiefly Science of Military Strategy and Science of Campaigns — are 13 to 20+ years out of date and no new editions are forthcoming, creating a widening analytical blind spot for U.S. planners trying to model Chinese responses to force-posture changes or strikes.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: The core thesis: as PLA doctrine ages without updates, U.S. wargaming and deterrence assessments rest on an increasingly stale foundation — a slow-building intelligence risk for anyone pricing US-China conflict scenarios.
insights.quote: This creates a “blind spot” for academics and “blue” force planners attempting to simulate how China would react to a fast-evolving battlefield environment.
insights.locator: This phenomenon is exacerbated by rapidly developing new technologies

insights.claim: On Feb. 8, 2025, China's Cyberspace Administration issued 'Measures for Managing the Dissemination of Military Information on the Internet,' banning online publication of PLA weapons-testing, drills, deployment, and unit organizational details, chilling the broader flow of Chinese military information to Western analysts.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: A dated, concrete regulatory event (Feb 2025) that analysts can point to as the start of a measurable degradation in PLA open-source information flow — useful as a before/after marker for source availability.
insights.locator: On Feb. 8, 2025, the Cyberspace Administration of China

insights.claim: The 2013 Academy of Military Sciences edition of Science of Military Strategy remains the most authoritative version available on Chinese strategic doctrine, and PLA interlocutors at a recent track-two dialogue told the author no new edition addressing the 2016 military reforms is forthcoming.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: Direct, sourced confirmation from PLA-linked interlocutors that the doctrinal update pipeline has stalled — removing hope that this gap self-resolves soon.
insights.locator: But during a track two dialogue I attended recently

insights.claim: Science of Campaigns, the sole authoritative PLA text on operational-level warfare, was written in 2006 and predates China's shift to 'informationized' and 'intelligentized' warfare, the advent of AI and autonomous systems, and the PLA's 2016 reorganization into joint theater commands.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: The one text Western analysts rely on for how the PLA actually fights campaigns is nearly two decades stale relative to China's AI/autonomy-driven doctrine shift, a direct gap for anyone assessing PLA operational concepts.
insights.locator: But Science of Campaigns was written over two decades ago

insights.claim: A Central Military Commission set of 'Guidelines on Joint Operations of the Chinese People's Liberation Army (Trial),' issued Nov. 13, 2020 to account for 'precision, intelligence, stealth, and unmanned weaponry,' has never been released in full text, leaving Western analysts to extrapolate PLA joint-operations thinking from secondary commentary.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: A named, dated document that would materially close the joint-operations doctrine gap exists but is withheld — a specific FOIA/diplomatic-ask target for U.S. analysts and officials.
insights.locator: Unfortunately, the People's Liberation Army has yet to make available

insights.claim: China's flagship strategic journal China Military Science has stopped public availability, and the PLA newspapers Air Force News and People's Navy also lost public access years ago, cutting off granular service-level reporting that once supplemented outdated doctrinal texts.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: Documents a broader pattern of shrinking granular open-source PLA reporting beyond the flagship doctrinal texts, reinforcing that the access problem is systemic, not a single-source issue.
insights.locator: But China Military Science, for example, appears to have stopped

insights.claim: In unclassified wargames, 'red team' players simulating China routinely default to mirror-imaging — advocating highly escalatory actions like targeting civilian populations or preemptive nuclear strikes — which contradicts the more calculated, defensive escalation posture reflected in actual PLA doctrine and historical record.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: A first-hand, RAND-experienced critique that current red-teaming overstates Chinese escalation risk via mirror-imaging — a caution for anyone using wargame outputs to price conflict-escalation scenarios.

insights.claim: In October 2021, the Financial Times revealed China had tested a nuclear-capable Fractional Orbital Bombardment System that surprised U.S. intelligence officials with its sophistication and its ability to evade missile defenses by lingering in orbit before de-orbiting onto unconventional trajectories, compressing adversary warning time.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: A concrete, sourced instance where the doctrinal-information gap plausibly contributed to U.S. intelligence being caught off guard by a Chinese weapons capability — the strongest evidentiary anchor in the piece for the cost of reduced access.
insights.locator: In October 2021, the Financial Times reported

insights.claim: China's rapid recent nuclear weapons buildup has fueled unresolved debate over whether Beijing has shifted its nuclear strategy toward preemptive use or coercive signaling — a question the author says the community is under-equipped to answer given the paucity of updated authoritative PLA doctrine.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: Flags China's nuclear-doctrine trajectory (preemption vs. coercion) as a live, high-stakes open question directly tied to the doctrinal-access gap — a key uncertainty for nuclear-risk pricing.
insights.locator: There are other cases, especially regarding the scale and speed

insights.claim: Morris invokes Robert Jervis's Perception and Misperception in International Politics to argue that because states act on their perceptions of reality rather than objective conditions, the U.S.-China doctrinal information gap directly raises the odds of deterrence failure, escalation, and war.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: Provides the theoretical throughline connecting a narrow information-access problem to macro escalation risk — useful lens for framing US-China tail-risk scenarios.
insights.locator: This brings to mind the central thesis of Robert Jervis's

insights.claim: Morris cautions that PLA doctrinal texts are 'guideposts, not predictors' of Chinese military behavior, tempering his own call for restored access with a warning against over-reading doctrine as a forecasting tool.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: A built-in caveat against overconfident doctrine-based forecasting — relevant for how much weight analysts should place on newly obtained PLA texts even if access is restored.
insights.quote: They are guideposts, not predictors, of behavior.
insights.locator: Finally, I am not implying that People's Liberation Army doctrinal texts

themes.theme: Proposed fix: reviving open-source intelligence infrastructure

insights.claim: Morris proposes reconstituting the Cold War-era Foreign Broadcast Information Service as a public 'National Foreign Information Repository' housed at the Library of Congress, migrating the CIA's Open Source Enterprise out of CIA governance to restore public translation and dissemination of PLA and other foreign military materials.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: The paper's central policy ask — a Library of Congress-based FOBIS successor — is a concrete legislative target (Intelligence Authorization Act or NDAA rider) worth tracking for anyone in open-source intelligence, translation services, or China-research adjacent markets.
insights.quote: it is time to bring the Open Source Enterprise, formally called the Foreign Broadcast Information Service, back into the public domain
insights.locator: That is why it is time to advocate

insights.claim: Public access to FBIS-descended translations ended in stages: the CIA terminated the World News Connection (the university/library distribution channel) in 2013, then in 2016 folded the Open Source Center into its Directorate of Digital Innovation as the 'Open Source Enterprise,' closing it to non-government users.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: Establishes the precise 2013-2016 timeline of public-access closure, giving a factual baseline for how long the current gap has existed and how a restoration bill might be scoped.
insights.locator: Final closure of public access to the Open Source Center

insights.claim: Morris argues AI and machine translation cannot substitute for a revived FBIS because the harder task is contextualizing PLA-specific jargon and institutional references and distinguishing authoritative doctrine from speculative commentary — work he says still requires human analysts.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: A direct counter to the 'AI will solve translation' assumption — implies durable demand for human China-military analysts and curated OSINT services even as raw machine translation improves.
insights.quote: translation is only one component of the analytic process
insights.locator: Finally, AI cannot fully replace the functions

insights.claim: Morris's proposal would require congressional action — via a new Intelligence Authorization Act or as part of a larger National Defense Authorization Act — to formally restructure the Foreign Broadcast Information Service from CIA to Library of Congress governance.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: Names the specific legislative vehicles that would need to move for this reform, a concrete marker to watch in future NDAA/IAA text.
insights.locator: The answer for the first concern is easy

insights.claim: Morris explicitly places primary blame for the doctrinal-access gap on the PLA's own information restrictions rather than U.S. policy, and cautions that even new Chinese doctrinal texts, if released, 'might not necessarily alleviate' the broader U.S.-China security dilemma.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: Tempers the proposal's expected upside — restoring access is framed as an analytical, not a security-dilemma-resolving, fix.
insights.locator: To be clear at the outset, as will be discussed

themes.theme: Structural drivers of the analyst gap

insights.claim: Demand for Chinese military analysis is rising even as authoritative source material shrinks, reflected in the proliferation of consulting firms dedicated to studying the Chinese 'way of war' amid DoD's designation of China as America's 'pacing threat.'
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: Identifies a widening supply/demand mismatch in China-military analysis — a tailwind for firms and analysts who can differentiate on genuine source access rather than commentary alone.
insights.locator: The irony is that Chinese military studies have never been in more demand

insights.claim: University-affiliated PLA research centers (NDU's Center for the Study of Chinese Military Affairs, the Naval War College's China Maritime Studies Institute, Air University's China Aerospace Studies Institute, Marine Corps University's China Military Studies Review, Army War College's China Landpower Studies Center) and FFRDCs like RAND and CNA still produce PLA research and translations, but increasingly restrict these outputs from public release.
insights.speaker: Lyle Morris
insights.so_what: Even the institutions best positioned to fill the doctrinal gap are pulling their output out of public view, meaning the private/classified analyst community is capturing value that used to be a public good.
insights.locator: Finally, U.S. military service-affiliated research institutes
